coredata.dc:title: Discovery of specific activity of 2-hydroxypentanoic acid acting on the mPR alpha (Paqr7) from the marine algae Padina
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Amin, Mohammad Tohidul
Acharjee, Mrityunjoy
Sarwar Jyoti, Md Maisum
Rezanujjaman, Md
Hassan, Mohammad Maksudul
Hossain, Md Forhad
Ahamed, Saokat
Kodani, Shinya
Tokumoto, Toshinobu
coredata.dc:description: Membrane progesterone receptors (mPRs) are members of the progestin and adipoQ (PAQR) receptor family that are stimulated by endogenous steroids to initiate rapid intracellular signaling through a nongenomic pathway. Previously, water-soluble compounds with mPRα binding activity from the marine algae Padina arborescens were fractionated by HPLC. Nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy revealed that the major component of the HPLC fraction was 2-hydroxypentanoic acid (2-HPA). In this study, the physiological activity of 2-HPA and its analogues was investigated using in vitro goldfish and in vivo zebrafish oocyte maturation and ovulation assays. Only 2-HPA showed inhibitory activity on oocyte maturation and ovulation of fish oocytes. The inhibitory activity of 2-HPA was compared between S- and R-type 2-HPA. The results showed that both types had the same level of activity. Furthermore, the interaction of 2-HPA with mPRα was analyzed by binding assay. 2-HPAs showed a substantial competitive binding affinity for the human membrane progesterone receptor α (hmPRα) in the graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-hmPRα binding assay. In contrast, synthetic structural analogues of 2-HPA showed no competitive binding activity. These results indicate that 2-HPA is a novel mPRα antagonist, and its chemical structure is highly restricted to show its activity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Membrane progesterone receptor
2-Hydroxypentanoic acid
Padina
Marine algae
Antagonist
Oocyte maturation

Identification of the major component in the Peak2 from Padina secretions by NMR experiment
Evaluation of the specific binding of 2-HPA and its analogues to mPRα
In vitro goldfish oocyte maturation assay
In vitro and in vivo oocyte maturation assay in zebrafish

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We thank Mr. Daijiro Tokumoto for the cultivation of goldfish. This study was supported by Grants-in-Aid for Scientific Research in Priority Areas from the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and JSPS KAKENHI Grant Number 23K05830 (to TT). We also acknowledge student scholarship from the Japanese Government (MEXT) Scholarship for MT Amin, MF. Hossain and S. Hossain; Honda Benjiro to MMS. Jyoti; Bangabandhu Science and Technology Fellowship Trust, Ministry of Science and Technology, Government of the People\u2019s Republic of Bangladesh for S Ahamed (memo no:08//04-01-2021).

We thank Mr. Daijiro Tokumoto for the cultivation of goldfish. This study was supported by Grants-in-Aid for Scientific Research in Priority Areas from the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and JSPS KAKENHI Grant Number 23K05830(to TT). We also acknowledge student scholarship from the Japanese Government (MEXT) Scholarship for MT Amin, MF. Hossain and S. Hossain; Honda Benjiro to MMS. Jyoti; Bangabandhu Science and Technology Fellowship Trust, Ministry of Science and Technology, Government of the People's Republic of Bangladesh for S Ahamed (memo no:08//04-01-2021).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: In vitro oocyte maturation assay of 2-HPA and its analogues using goldfish. (A) Structure of 2-HPA isomers and their analogues. Chemical structures of two isomers of 2-HPA and its analogues used in this study. Chemical structures were drawn by the Swiss target Prediction (http://www.swisstargetprediction.ch/). (B) In vitro oocyte maturation assay of 2-HPA and its analogues. 2-HPA and its analogues were added to indicate concentrations, and then maturation was induced by 1 μM of 17,20β-DHP (+DHP 1). After 6 h of incubation, oocytes with or without germinal vesicle breakdown (GVBD) were counted, and the percentage of GVBD was calculated. As a negative control, oocytes were incubated with 0.1 % ethanol (EtOH) or 1 μM of DHP alone as a positive control (DHP 1). The assay was performed in triplicate, and the average percentage of GVBD was expressed as the standard deviation.

In vivo oocyte maturation and ovulation assay of 2-HPA and its analogues using zebrafish. The antagonistic activity of 2-HPA and its analogues against oocyte maturation and ovulation induction was analyzed by in vivo treatment in zebrafish. 2-HPA and its analogues were added into the water at 0.1 μM, and then maturation and ovulation were induced by adding 0.1 μM of 17,20β-DHP (DHP 0.1). After 4 h of treatment with compounds by addition to water, %GVBD (closed column) and %ovulation (open column) were determined by scoring the oocytes that had become transparent and formed an egg membrane by egg activation. Fish were incubated with 0.01 % ethanol (EtOH) as a negative control or with 0.1 μM DHP alone as a positive control (DHP 0.1). Three fish were used per treatment. Means of data are presented with standard deviation. Asterisks represent significant differences between DHP alone and DHP with 2-HPA treatment (∗∗P ≤ 0.001).

In vitro and in vivo assay of (S) and (R) types of 2-HPA using zebrafish. (A) The in vitro assay results for the (S) and (R) types of 2-HPA are shown. Zebrafish oocytes were used for this low-number assay. Each compound was added to the zebrafish Ringer's solution at a final concentration of 1 μM with 1 μM DHP. After 2 h of incubation, the percentage of germinal vesicle breakdown (%GVBD: indicated by the closed column) was determined by scoring the oocytes that became transparent. The assay was performed in triplicate on three fish. The mean of triplicate data is presented with standard deviation. Asterisks indicate significant differences between DHP alone and DHP with 2-HPA treatments (∗P ≤ 0.05). (B) The in vivo assay results for the (S) and (R) types of 2-HPA are shown. Each compound was added to the water at a final concentration of 0.1 μM with 0.1 μM DHP. After 4 h of incubation, %GVBD (closed column) and %ovulation (open column) were determined by scoring the oocytes as they became transparent and formed an egg membrane. Three fish per treatment were used. Means of data are presented with standard deviation. Asterisks indicate significant differences between DHP alone and DHP with 2-HPA treatments (∗∗P ≤ 0.001).

GQD-hmPRα binding assay of 2-HPA and its analogues. Competition of the binding of P4-BSA-FITC with GQD-hmPRα by 2-HPA and its analogues. The dose-dependent effects of steroids (progesterone, estradiol-17β), (S)-type and (R)-type of 2-HPA, purified fraction from Padina (Peak-2) and 2-HPA analogues (see Fig. 1A) were studied. Measurements were conducted three times, and the means of data were presented with standard deviation.

head.ce:title: Discovery of specific activity of 2-hydroxypentanoic acid acting on the mPR alpha (Paqr7) from the marine algae Padina

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Bioscience, Graduate School of Science and Technology, National University Corporation, Shizuoka University, 836 Ohya, Suruga-ku, Shizuoka, 422-8529, Japan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Graduate School of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Bioscience, Graduate School of Science and Technology, National University Corporation, Shizuoka University, 836 Ohya, Suruga-ku, Shizuoka, 422-8529, Japan

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors contributed equally to this study.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Membrane progesterone receptors (mPRs) are members of the progestin and adipoQ (PAQR) receptor family that are stimulated by endogenous steroids to initiate rapid intracellular signaling through a nongenomic pathway. Previously, water-soluble compounds with mPRα binding activity from the marine algae Padina arborescens were fractionated by HPLC. Nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy revealed that the major component of the HPLC fraction was 2-hydroxypentanoic acid (2-HPA).

In this study, the physiological activity of 2-HPA and its analogues was investigated using in vitro goldfish and in vivo zebrafish oocyte maturation and ovulation assays. Only 2-HPA showed inhibitory activity on oocyte maturation and ovulation of fish oocytes. The inhibitory activity of 2-HPA was compared between S- and R-type 2-HPA. The results showed that both types had the same level of activity. Furthermore, the interaction of 2-HPA with mPRα was analyzed by binding assay. 2-HPAs showed a substantial competitive binding affinity for the human membrane progesterone receptor α (hmPRα) in the graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-hmPRα binding assay. In contrast, synthetic structural analogues of 2-HPA showed no competitive binding activity.
These results indicate that 2-HPA is a novel mPRα antagonist, and its chemical structure is highly restricted to show its activity.

ce:section.ce:para: The progesterone membrane receptor (mPR) was discovered as a candidate membrane receptor molecule for fish oocyte maturation-induced steroids [1]. The action of steroid hormones has generally been through a “genomic action,” a pathway mediated by nuclear receptors present in the cell. In this response, hormone-receptor complexes bind to genomic DNA in the nucleus and exert their hormonal effects by promoting the expression of specific genes. In contrast, mPRs localize to the cell surface and mediate a “non-genomic action” that does not involve gene expression [2,3]. In this response, when the hormone binds to the receptor outside the cell, signaling molecules inside the cell activate specific proteins to exert a hormonal effect [4]. Studies in goldfish and zebrafish have shown that DES, an endocrine disruptor with a structure similar to that of the oocyte maturation inducing hormone and a potential endocrine disrupting effect, binds to mPRα as the oocyte maturation inducing hormone and causes endocrine disruption. Org OD-02, which was developed as an mPR-selective agonist, also shows oocyte maturation-inducing effects in fish, suggesting that mPR-responsive substances do indeed exert physiological effects [5]. A brain progestin, allopregnanolone, is attracting attention as a therapeutic drug for Alzheimer's disease [6]. Recently, it has been suggested that the antiapoptotic effects of allopregnanolone on neuronal cells are mediated by mPRs [7]. In addition, allopregnanolone has been shown to promote the development of chick Purkinje cells via mPRα [8]. Thus, compounds that act on the mPR molecule, which is emerging as the receptor for a novel pathway of progestin action, are potential candidates for new drugs, and we have developed a screening system for substances that act on the mPR [9]. We have established a method using cultured cells and have recently established a simpler assay using the mPR molecule itself, which has been successfully expressed and purified using yeast [10,11]. In a previous study, we demonstrated the presence of mPR-reactive substances in the seawater of the Mauritius coral reef [12]. We used established assays to purify the mPR-reactive substances from secreted compounds of the marine alga Padina arborescens [13]. The active fraction was shown to contain 2-hydroxypentanoic acid (2-HPA) as the major component. Therefore, we tested the reactivity of 2-HPA against mPRα and confirmed that it has physiological activity to inhibit the induction of oocyte maturation, indicating that 2-HPA is a novel mPRα antagonist.

Chemicals were purchased from companies accordingly: (S)-2-HPA (Combi-Blocks, San Diego, CA); (R)-2-HPA (AMATEK CHEMISTRY, Hong Kong); 2-hydroxybutyric acid (Tokyo Kasei, Japan), valeric acid, 2-hydroxyhexanoic acid, progesterone and 17β-estradiol (Sigma-Aldrich); 1,2-pentanediol, and 3-hydroxyvaleric acid (AdooQ BioScience, Irvine, CA).
Goldfish were reared and maintained under standard laboratory conditions. The fish used in the experiments were kept in a flow-through aquarium at 20–25 °C under a 14-h light/10-h dark cycle.

Zebrafish were reared and maintained under standard laboratory conditions. The fish used in the experiments were maintained in a flow-through culture system at 28.5 °C under a 14-h light/10-h dark cycle.

All fish experiments were approved by the Institutional Ethics Committee of Shizuoka University, Japan (approval numbers 2022F-3 and 2023F-9), and the guidelines for using animals established by this committee were strictly adhered to. The study was carried out in compliance with the ARRIVE guidelines [14].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Separation of components in Padina secretions by HPLC
ce:section.ce:para: Sampling and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) purification were performed as previously described [13]. Two peaks (Peak1 and 2) were obtained in the final purification step. The Peak2 fraction was dried using a freeze dryer (FDU-810, EYELA). The dried compounds were dissolved in ethanol, which was used for GQD-hmPRα binding assay and physiological activity assays.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification of the major component in the Peak2 from Padina secretions by NMR experiment
ce:section.ce:para: The Peak2 obtained in the HPLC separation was analyzed by NMR. The fraction of Peak2 was lyophilized and then dissolved in 500 μL of CDCl3. The NMR spectra, including 1H, 13C, DQF-COSY, TOCSY, HMQC and HMBC, were measured using a JNM-ECZ500R spectrometer (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan), according to the manufacturer's instructions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the specific binding of 2-HPA and its analogues to mPRα
ce:section.ce:para: The binding properties of 2-HPA and its analogues to mPRα were evaluated by GQD-hmPRα according to the method described previously [10].
Competitive mPRα binding activity was determined as previously described by using GQD conjugated purified hmPRα and [3H]-labeled fish maturation-inducing hormone 17,20β-DHP [15].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro goldfish oocyte maturation assay
ce:section.ce:para: An in vitro oocyte maturation assay was performed on female goldfish as previously described [16].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro and in vivo oocyte maturation assay in zebrafish
ce:section.ce:para: An in vitro and in vivo zebrafish oocyte maturation and ovulation assay was performed on female zebrafish as previously described [5,17].

Results were represented as Mean ± SD (Standard Deviation). Data significance values were calculated by using paired t-test. All analytical data and graphs were prepared on GraphPad Prism software version 9.4.0 for Mac OS (GraphPad Software, San Diego, California, USA).

In a previous study, compounds with mPRα-binding activity from secretion material of the marine algae Padina arborescens were fractionated as two peaks by HPLC steps [13]. Compounds in both peaks (Peak1 and Peak2) showed competitive binding activity against human mPRα and inhibitory activity on fish oocyte maturation and ovulation. We attempted to analyze the chemical structure of the active compound by chemical analysis. Peak2 was subjected to NMR analysis using 1H NMR, 13C NMR, DQF-COSY, TOCSY, HMQC, and HMBC (Figs. S1–3). Analysis of the NMR data indicated that Peak2 was a mixture of several compounds. As a result of the analysis of the 2D NMR data, the main component was determined to have the carbon skeleton of 2-hydroxypentanoic acid (2-HPA). The results prompted us to investigate 2-HPA and analogous compounds for the activity.

The physiological activities of 2-HPA and its analogues were evaluated by an in vitro oocyte maturation assay using fish oocytes. Oocyte meiotic maturation is induced by mPRα-mediated signal transduction through nongenomic action as a biological process, making a discovery of mPRα [18]. Female goldfish oocytes were used for large-scale analysis. 17α,20β-dihydroxy-4-prognen-3-one (DHP), is a natural agonist for the mPRs and induces oocyte maturation by binding to the mPRs on the oocyte cell surface.

Subsequently, the mPRα binding activity of 2-HPA and its analogues was evaluated using the newly established GQD-hmPRα binding assay, which allows high throughput screening of mPRα-interacting compounds. As shown in Fig. 4 , the Peak2 fraction isolated from Padina (Peak2) and pure (S)-2-HPA decreased fluorescence intensity in a concentration-dependent manner comparable to the positive control, progesterone. In contrast, no decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed for analogues of 2-HPA, such as the negative control estradiol. Among the 2-HPA and its analogues tested in this study, only 2-HPA has hmPRα binding properties, suggesting that the binding of 2-HPA to the hmPRα is specific and that the interaction is conformationally restricted.

The calculated IC50 values for peak2 and (S)-2-HPA were 1.7 and 2.1 nM, respectively. Competitive binding activities were also confirmed by a binding assay using the radioisotope-labeled progestin, [3H]-17α,20β-dihydroxy-4-pregnen-3-one ([3H]-17,20β-DHP) (Fig. 4). Both R & S forms of 2-HPA competed for binding of [3H]-17,20β-DHP to GQD-hmPRα. The result indicated that 2-HPA possessed hmPRα-interacting activity as the peak2 fraction.

This study identified a novel natural compound from Padina arborescence that interacts with mPRα. In GQD-hmPRα binding assay, the compound in peak 2 showed higher affinity than progesterone. Synthetic compounds identified as a major component of Peak2, 2-HPA, showed higher affinity than Peak2 (Fig. 4). Corresponding to this high affinity, 2-HPA showed inhibitory activity against fish oocyte maturation and ovulation at the same concentration of progestin (Figs. 1A, 2 and 3).

We investigated the structure-activity relationship of 2-HPA analogues to identify the key residues that interact with mPRα. Interestingly, none of the analogues showed activity. The results showed that the hydroxide at position 2 is essential for interaction with mPRα. The length of the carbon chain is also crucial for binding to mPRα. Thus, it is suggested that the relatively simple structure of 2-HPA is a strict fit for the steroid-binding site of mPRα. Conversely, the structural isomers of 2-HPA, (S)- 2-HPA and (R)- 2-HPA showed no difference in activity (Fig. 4). The results suggest that hydrogens binding to carbon 5 do not contribute to any binding with mPRα.

Treatment with 2-HPA resulted in the prevention of oocyte maturation and ovulation in fish. It can be concluded that the prevention of fish oocyte maturation is due to the binding of 2-HPA to mPRα. However, we cannot exclude the possibility that the inhibition of ovulation is due to the binding of 2-HPA to the nuclear progesterone receptor, which induces ovulation [19]. We believe that this inhibition is due to inhibition of the induction of oocyte maturation, which is the first response in the sequential induction of oocyte maturation and ovulation.

In this study, we identified the first antagonist of mPRα, the first molecule discovered to mediate a steroid nongenomic action. mPR has five subtypes, and in addition to its function in the reproductive system, which is the subject of this study, mPRα has been implicated in mediating steroid nongenomic actions in the brain and other parts of the body, and in regulating various biological processes. Antagonists of mPRα may be useful in validating these functions and may also be candidates for new drugs. The identification of novel substances from seaweeds, especially water soluble substances, is currently underway. It is highly likely that new antagonists and agonists of mPR will be discovered among these substances. Further elucidation of novel compounds should be encouraged in the future.

Mohammad Tohidul Amin: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Mrityunjoy Acharjee: Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Md Maisum Sarwar Jyoti: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Md Rezanujjaman: Formal analysis, Data curation. Mohammad Maksudul Hassan: Formal analysis, Data curation. Md Forhad Hossain: Formal analysis, Data curation. Saokat Ahamed: Formal analysis, Data curation. Shinya Kodani: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Toshinobu Tokumoto: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.
Not applicable.
The datasets generated and/or analyzed during the current study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.
All authors review and approve the manuscript for publication.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that there is not conflict of interest that could be perceived as prejudicing the impartiality of the research reported.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank Mr. Daijiro Tokumoto for the cultivation of goldfish. This study was supported by Grants-in-Aid for Scientific Research in Priority Areas from the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and JSPS KAKENHI Grant Number 23K05830(to TT). We also acknowledge student scholarship from the Japanese Government (MEXT) Scholarship for MT Amin, MF. Hossain and S. Hossain; Honda Benjiro to MMS. Jyoti; Bangabandhu Science and Technology Fellowship Trust, Ministry of Science and Technology, Government of the People's Republic of Bangladesh for S Ahamed (memo no:08//04-01-2021).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cloning, expression, and characterization of a membrane progestin receptor and evidence it is an intermediary in meiotic maturation of fish oocytes
Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. U. S. A.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Zhu, C.D. Rice, Y. Pang, M. Pace, P. Thomas, Cloning, expression, and characterization of a membrane progestin receptor and evidence it is an intermediary in meiotic maturation of fish oocytes, Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A 100 (2003) 2231-2236.

Endocrinology

Neurotherapeutics

J. Mol. Neurosci.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Diethylstilbestrol induces fish oocyte maturation
Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. U.S.A.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Tokumoto, M. Tokumoto, R. Horiguchi, K. Ishikawa, Y. Nagahama, Diethylstilbestrol induces fish oocyte maturation, Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America 101 (2004) 3686-3690.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In vivo induction of oocyte maturation and ovulation in zebrafish
PLoS One

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Tokumoto, T. Yamaguchi, S. Ii, M. Tokumoto, In Vivo Induction of Oocyte Maturation and Ovulation in Zebrafish, Plos One 6 (2011) 1-6.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Identification, classification, and partial characterization of genes in humans and other vertebrates homologous to a fish membrane progestin receptor
Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. U. S. A.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Zhu, J. Bond, P. Thomas, Identification, classification, and partial characterization of genes in humans and other vertebrates homologous to a fish membrane progestin receptor, Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A 100 (2003) 2237-2242.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Progestin and nuclear progestin receptor are essential for upregulation of metalloproteinase in zebrafish preovulatory follicles
Front. Endocrinol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D.T. Liu, N.J. Carter, X.J. Wu, W.S. Hong, S.X. Chen, Y. Zhu, Progestin and Nuclear Progestin Receptor Are Essential for Upregulation of Metalloproteinase in Zebrafish Preovulatory Follicles, Frontiers in Endocrinology 9 (2018).
